system_prompt:
你是时尚资讯主笔，负责输出一条 digest 的长文。

输入包括 digest 计划与对应的 article 摘要/引用。你的任务是生成完整中文稿件：
- title_zh、dek_zh 为中文标题与导语
- body_markdown 为简洁可读的 Markdown 正文（不要包含 JSON）
- source_article_ids 必须来自输入

规则：
- 只基于输入事实，不要编造
- 输出必须是严格 JSON，符合 schema
- 不要输出解释文本或 Markdown 代码块

输出 JSON 结构：
{
  "title_zh": "...",
  "dek_zh": "...",
  "body_markdown": "...",
  "source_article_ids": ["..."]
}

messages.content:
{"plan": {"business_date": "2026-03-29", "facet": "brand_market", "story_keys": ["2b0daec7-6070-44a0-828b-8e1d30a8a32f"], "article_ids": ["76fd2353-2cb8-471c-90a9-156ce3176d51"], "editorial_angle": "解析男性配饰消费趋势的微妙变化，从Acne Studios Camero包的设计渊源切入，探讨当代男性对日常包袋的功能与风格双重需求", "title_zh": "男士包袋潮流转向：Acne Studios Camero取代托特包成为新宠", "dek_zh": "时尚男性正抛弃托特包，转而选择Acne Studios的Camero包款，这一趋势背后反映着实用性与设计感的微妙平衡", "source_names": ["GQ"]}, "source_articles": [{"article_id": "76fd2353-2cb8-471c-90a9-156ce3176d51", "source_name": "GQ", "title_raw": "Why Trendsetting Dudes Keep Ditching Their Totes for This Designer Bag", "summary_raw": "After decades of tote bag dominance, stylish guys are skipping the backpacks and briefcases in favor of one alternative: Acne Studios' omnipresent Camero.", "body_markdown": "# Why Trendsetting Dudes Keep Ditching Their Totes for This Designer Bag

After decades of tote bag dominance, stylish guys are skipping the backpacks and briefcases in favor of one alternative: Acne Studios' omnipresent Camero.

When I asked model Buchi Okafor what he'd do if he lost his Acne Studios Camero bag, his answer was telling. “I'd be upset for, like, ten minutes," Okafor said. “And then I'd be back online trying to find another one.” You don't say that unless you really, really like it, especially for something that costs a not-insignificant amount of cash.

And right now, the Camero is everywhere. Not in a forced, overly marketed way, but in that organic way where it just keeps popping up. On Instagram. On TikTok. Even on BlueSky (remember that?). It's usually worn crossbody on fashion guys, sitting low against a pair of baggy jeans, maybe a boxy jacket up top. It doesn't dominate the whole fit, but it always feels like the finishing touch.

I first noticed it late last year, then again during fashion week, and now it feels like it's hit that tipping point where you start seeing it out in the wild as much as you do on your FYP. Not just on one type of guy, either. Sure, you'll spot it on the usual menswearheads, but also on guys wearing everything from suits to sweats. I saw a fella in Milan wearing it with a football jersey and Fila trackpants. It's not locked into one look, which raises the obvious question: what's so great about this bag?

Part of it is timing. The Camero landed as part of Acne Studios' spring-summer 2025 collection, inspired by a bag Acne creative director Jonny Johansson once used early in his career to carry his Polaroid camera and lens.

“Camera bags”, though, have been around for decades. Originally, they were compact, boxy-looking things built for actual, well, cameras—structured, practical, usually with multiple compartments to keep all your gear nice and organized. Fashion picked up on that functionality years ago, softening the edges, and turning it into something you can actually wear every day without owning a Leica.

Acne's take doesn't reinvent the wheel, but it tweaks it just enough. “It's super practical to use and you're going to look sexy wearing it. What's there not to love?” says content creator Bryan Leon. And he's right. Most bags tend to make you choose form and function—either you get something that actually carries all your stuff or something that looks sick on the 'gram. The Camero, somehow, manages to do both without breaking a sweat.

Its shape keeps coming up as something else that really sells it. “It almost has two personalities,” Okafor says. “When it's empty, it slouches in this really effortless way. Then once you actually pack it out, you get that more structured, vintage camera bag feel.” That idea of it shifting depending on how you use it is probably a big part of why it works across so many different styles.

And people are noticing. Nearly everyone I spoke to had some version of the same story. Leon gets stopped in London's SoHo by strangers asking where it's from. Frank Johnson, a sneakerhead who's deep into anime, picks up compliments while out on shoots. And according to content creator Justin Park, even when it's not happening IRL, it's happening online. “It's connected me with so many people who share the same taste in design through social media, which I never expected,” he says.

That's where the biggest shift comes through. For the longest time, dudes carrying bags outside of backpacks or gym duffels still felt like a bit of a thing. Not controversial, exactly, but not fully normalized either. But now, that weird taboo is finally gone. “I think guys are gravitating to the Camero because it genuinely looks like a gender neutral bag,” says Dominic Grizzelle, a stylist based in LA. “No dainty detailing, a crossbody strap, numerous pockets. It's basically everything us men ask for in a bag.” He even called it “the modern-day briefcase,” which, when you think about it, is exactly what it is.

The Camero isn't the first camera bag to come out of a luxury house. Bottega Veneta, Loewe, and Saint Laurent have all done their own ones, and Lemaire's Gear Bag had a proper moment too, especially among quiet luxury types. But what sets the Camero apart is that it feels a bit more… unexpected. It's not just a straight copy-and-paste of the classic shape—there's more personality to it. Even the little key charm adds something, a small detail that stops it from feeling too serious.

Plus, there's the fact that it doesn't scream. No massive branding, no overly chunky zips. Johnson summed that up nicely. He's into fashion that “can make a statement without having the need to slap logos all over it.” It's the kinda bag you clock if you know, and ignore if you don't, which is exactly where a lot of menswear is sitting right now.

Of course, none of this comes cheap. The Camero starts at $1,650 for the smaller version and climbs to over $4,000 for the larger “Hotel” size, which puts it firmly in that space where you have to at least pause before entering your credit card details. It's not an impulse buy, and it's definitely not the kind of thing you pick up just because your algorithm showed it to you during your nightly doomscroll.

But according to the men I spoke to, it's worth every single penny. Grizzelle's already thinking about doubling up, eyeing the bigger size as a carry-on. “I think it's just a great bag for every kind of guy,” he says. So is it the most wanted bag in menswear right now? It's definitely up there. If somebody's willing to replace it ten minutes after losing it, that probably tells you everything you need to know.

This story originally appeared on British GQ.
"}]}
